verbatim_text: Okay. So our second question. Also okay. So this paper was also one of the first papers to use activation patching or interchange interventions which is now a really widely used technique for causal tracing and interpretability research. We were also the first to show that language models actually built these latent world models which has since inspired a number of really influential follow ups including this work on extracting board states from language models trained on Othello games. Okay. So we just saw language models were going to use internal world models. How did they actually do so? So how are language models doing this sort of thing, this sort of procedure in their internals? So inferring the state of the world from the first input can be quite easy. You can just memorize a lookup table from input to state. Similarly predicting the set of valid outputs from a state can also be a lookup table. But the real crux, the real hard part of this procedure is how they're able to do a series of update steps in order to predict the final state. So this is known as the state tracking problem.
